$ 1. That the Governor of the Commonwealth of Governorto

or stream in thisState for the purpose of improving

classification.title: Amendment to statute regarding student appointments to the Agricultural and Mechanical College
classification.summary: This statute amends a previous law to specify that each county in Kentucky is entitled to appoint one white pupil annually to the Agricultural and Mechanical College for every three thousand white school children, and one for each fraction thereof over fifteen hundred, based on the last official census. Each county is guaranteed at least one appointment. The appointed students receive free tuition, matriculation fees, room rent, fuel, and lights, along with other university advantages, except for board.

It also outlines the process for selecting beneficiaries and the duties of County Superintendents. Additionally, it grants the Board of Trustees discretion to offer free tuition to students preparing for the ministry within any white religious denomination. Furthermore, it mandates that the Board of Trustees be bi-partisan after January 1, 1910, with the Governor ensuring equal representation from the two leading political parties.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 2057 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to Adulteration and Misbranding of Food and Drugs
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Statutes Section 2057 to define what constitutes adulterated or misbranded articles of food. classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning the Normal Department of the State University. It eliminates the existing Normal Department and establishes a new Department of Education with collegiate rank, leading to a degree in pedagogy. This degree will allow holders to teach in common and high schools without further examination, subject to approval by the State Superintendent of Public Instruction. The act also details appropriation and reporting requirements for the university's improvements.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 4389 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to Certificates for Teachers
classification.summary: This act details the requirements and validity periods for elementary, intermediate, and advanced teaching certificates conferred by a university department. It outlines the process for extending advanced certificates for life based on teaching success and moral character, subject to approval by the State Superintendent of Public Instruction. The act also states that any certificate can be revoked for cause. Additionally, it mandates the elimination of Sub-Freshmen work from the university curriculum over two years, as educational conditions permit.
classification.reasoning: The statute focuses on the certification and curriculum of teachers within the Kentucky public school system. It outlines different levels of teaching certificates and their durations, as well as a process for extending advanced certificates. The text does not contain any explicit or implicit references to race or racial segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 684 of the Kentucky Statutes
classification.summary: This act amends Section 684 of the Kentucky Statutes concerning the incorporation of stock companies and mutual insurance companies. It reduces the minimum capital stock requirement for stock companies from one hundred thousand dollars to fifty thousand dollars and adjusts the requirements for mutual insurance companies regarding the number of applicants and premium amounts. classification.title: AN ACT to amend Section 2630 and 2635a of the Kentucky Statutes edited by the Hon . John D. Carroll and the edition of 1903 relating to the sale of drugs .
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Statutes sections 2630 and 2635a concerning the retail sale of poisons. It requires sellers to label poisons with the article's name, the word "poison," the seller's business information, and common antidotes. Sellers must also ensure poisons are for legitimate purposes and, for highly toxic substances, maintain a registry of sales including buyer's name and residence, article name, quantity, and intended purpose. This registry must be preserved for at least two years and be open for inspection.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the regulation of poison sales, including labeling requirements and the maintenance of a sales registry for certain highly toxic substances. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Sale of cocaine, &c., regulated.
classification.summary: This statute regulates the sale and dispensing of alkaloid cocaine, its salts, alpha or beta eucaine, their salts, or any admixture containing them. Such substances can only be sold or dispensed upon the original written prescription of a legally qualified physician, dentist, or veterinary surgeon. Prescriptions can only be filled once, and no copies can be taken. Registered pharmacists must record the quantity, patient name, and date on the prescription. Prescription files are open to inspection by the Kentucky Board of Pharmacy, its inspectors, or the county coroner. Failure to comply results in a misdemeanor charge with fines ranging from $10 to $100.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of controlled substances (cocaine and eucaine) and their dispensing. It outlines requirements for prescriptions and record-keeping for pharmacists. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 1784 of the Kentucky Statutes, relating to division fences.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Statute Section 1784 concerning the construction and maintenance of division fences between adjoining landowners. It clarifies the process for one party to require the other to build their portion of a fence, establishes a lien for the cost of construction if the other party fails to comply after notice, and outlines exceptions such as prior compensation or written agreements. It also includes a provision that nothing in this section shall conflict with an act requiring railroad corporations to fence their right of way and construct barriers.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend Section 78, of an Act entitled: "AN ACT for the government of cities of the fourth class."
classification.summary: This act amends Section 78 of an existing act concerning the government of cities of the fourth class. It grants the Board of Council the power to open, extend, widen, or close public ways, establish markets, and acquire necessary land through purchase or condemnation. It also outlines the procedure for closing public ways, requiring the City Attorney to initiate legal action and making owners of adjacent land defendants in the suit. The court will decree the closing if all competent defendants agree, or impanel a jury if there are objections or defendants under disability (excluding coverture).

It outlines procedures for opening, closing, and widening public ways, and establishing markets. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The procedures described are administrative and legal, applicable to all citizens and property owners within the city's jurisdiction. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to provide for two District Detectives for , and an Annual Expense Fund of $500.00 for the Commonwealth's Attorney of Districts containing counties in the Commonwealth , containing a population of 150,000 or over.
classification.summary: This act creates the offices of two District Detectives for Commonwealth's Attorneys in districts with counties having a population of 150,000 or over. These detectives are appointed by the Commonwealth's Attorney, have a four-year term (removable at will), and are tasked with assisting in criminal case preparation by investigating evidence and facts. They possess the same powers of arrest and process execution as sheriffs. Each detective receives a salary of $1,000 per annum, paid from the county levy. Additionally, an annual expense fund of up to $500 is provided for the Commonwealth's Attorney.
classification.reasoning: The statute establishes a new law enforcement position, District Detectives, to assist Commonwealth's Attorneys in populous counties. The text focuses on the creation, duties, powers, and salary of these positions. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The criteria for the application of this law are solely based on county population. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the erection and maintenance of levees in the counties of Ballard, Carlisle, Fulton and Hickman," approved March 17, 1896, so as to add two new sub-sections thereto, to be designated as sub-sections 12 and 13.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning levees in Ballard, Carlisle, Fulton, and Hickman counties. It introduces two new sub-sections: Sub-section 12 makes it unlawful for hogs to run at large on public levees, with penalties for owners and provisions for impounding and selling offending hogs. Sub-section 13 grants levee commissioners the power to levy an annual tax, not exceeding 50 cents per one hundred dollars of property value, on property within the protected territory to fund levee construction and repair. This tax is to be assessed and collected by the county assessor and sheriff in the same manner as county revenues.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses regulations concerning hogs on levees and the taxation for levee maintenance. classification.summary: This act appropriates $15,000 to the Kentucky Institution for Feeble Minded Children for the purpose of building an addition to accommodate more pupils. It also directs the Board of Control to use $2,500 to pay off an existing note for the institution. The money is to be drawn on the draft of the Kentucky State Board of Control, and the President of the Board is to advertise for bids for labor and materials.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns appropriations for an institution for "Feeble Minded Children" and the payment of a debt. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any other element typically associated with Jim Crow laws. The focus is purely on funding and construction for the institution. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with high confidence. The category is 'education' as it pertains to an institution for children. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' and the year 1908.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend and re-enact certain sections of the Kentucky Statutes, Chapter 39, Article5, relating to cities of the fourth class.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Statutes Section 3572, Article 5, Subdivision 9, Chapter 89, concerning cities of the fourth class. It allows for the original construction and improvement of streets, roads, alleys, market places, lanes, public squares or grounds, wharves, levees, or avenues. These improvements, including grading, macadamizing, paving, curbing, guttering, sidewalks, and sewers, can be done at the exclusive cost of the owners of the lots or land fronting or bordering the improved grounds, as prescribed by specifications and ordinances.

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 3573, Article 5, Subdivision 9, Chapter 89, of the Kentucky Statutes.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Statutes Section 3573, concerning procedures for municipal improvements. It clarifies how the cost of improvements can be apportioned among property owners, allows the city council to initiate improvements without a petition from owners if two-thirds of members concur, and outlines procedures for awarding contracts, including the requirement for a performance bond from the contractor. It also details the process for receiving completed work.

classification.title: Amendment to Kentucky Statutes, Section 3575, Article 5, Subdivision 9, Chapter 89, Regarding Street Improvement Bonds
classification.summary: This statute amends Section 3575 of the Kentucky Statutes, detailing the redemption of bonds issued for street improvements. It specifies that funds from bond sales must be kept in a separate 'Street Improvement Fund' managed by the city treasurer. The treasurer is responsible for disbursing these funds as directed by the city council for street improvements and must maintain a separate account for this fund, crediting any interest to it. The amount of bonds issued is tied to unpaid assessments and costs associated with bond issuance.
classification.reasoning: The statute text provided discusses the financial management and redemption of bonds for street improvements. It outlines procedures for handling funds, accounting, and the responsibilities of the city treasurer and council. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'amendments' as it explicitly states 'be, and the same is hereby amended and re-enacted'. The confidence is high because the content is purely financial and administrative, with no discernible racial component.

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 3572 of the Kentucky Statutes, relating to street improvements.
classification.summary: This act details the process for assessing and collecting costs for street improvements in cities. It specifies how costs, including intersections and crossings, are to be assessed equally by the front footage of bordering property. It also outlines how the city itself will pay for improvements on city-owned property or portions of improvements it assumes, using funds from the general fund or street improvement bonds. Assessments are to be made upon completion, placed on the tax list, and payable in ten equal annual installments with interest.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses the financial and procedural aspects of municipal street improvements. It outlines how costs are to be assessed against bordering properties and how the city itself will handle payments for improvements on its own land or assumed portions. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend and re-enact Section 3646 of the Kentucky Statutes.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 3646 of the Kentucky Statutes to allow cities of the fifth class to issue renewal or funding bonds to cover existing bonded and floating debt. It specifies conditions for these bonds, including not exceeding the principal debt, a maximum interest rate of six percent, and not being sold below par. The act also mandates an annual allocation to a sinking fund for interest payments and bond redemption, and directs that all moneys from licenses, poll tax, fines, penalties, and forfeitures be paid into the general fund.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with municipal finance, specifically the issuance of bonds to manage city debt. It does not contain any language, explicit or implicit, that suggests racial discrimination or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'ACT' from the 'GENERAL ASSEMBLY' in a specific year. The confidence is high because the content is clearly financial and administrative, with no discernible racial component.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the working of roads and the building and repairing of bridges in the several counties of this Commonwealth," approved March 17, 1876, and all acts amendatory thereof.
classification.summary: This act details the responsibilities of road supervisors, including reporting on road work, hired hands, and individuals working off fines or sentences. It outlines procedures for paying overseers and hands, and allows for the appointment of special commissioners for new roads. It also specifies that delinquent taxpayers shall be put on roads convenient to their residences and credited at a rate of one dollar per full day's work. The act also provides an alternative for Fiscal Courts to authorize the County Judge to let out road work and bridge construction instead of appointing a supervisor.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the administration and execution of road work, including the use of labor to pay off fines and sentences, and the treatment of delinquent taxpayers. While it deals with labor and potential penal consequences (working off fines/sentences), there is no explicit or implicit mention of race. The mechanisms described are general and would apply to all citizens. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' and the year 1908. The category is 'labor' due to the focus on work requirements and payment for services related to public works.

classification.summary: This act repeals sections 21, 22, 23, 24, and 25 of Article 8, Chapter 22 of the Acts of 1906. classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 29 and 30 of Article 8 of an Act Entitled "An Act to Provide for the Assessment and Collection of Taxes"
classification.summary: This act amends sections 29 and 30 of Article 8 of a tax assessment and collection act. It clarifies the county clerk's authority to collect delinquent taxes, interest, costs, commissions, and penalties for lands sold to the state, county, and district. The clerk is required to report these collections to the Auditor of Public Accounts, county treasurer, and district officer, and to pay collected funds into the respective treasuries. The County Judge is to examine the clerk's report. The act also mandates that sheriffs or collectors record tax, interest, and penalty collections from delinquents in their record books.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the administrative procedures for collecting delinquent taxes and the redemption of lands sold for unpaid taxes. It outlines the roles and responsibilities of county clerks, county judges, sheriffs, and tax officials in this process. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The procedures described are administrative and financial, not related to segregation or the restriction of rights based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 1861 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to the Duties of County Jailers and the Appropriation of County Funds for Public Property.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Statutes Section 1861 to clarify the responsibilities of county jailers in maintaining public property (courthouse, clerk's offices, jail, etc.) in counties with a population under seventy-five thousand. It mandates that the Fiscal Court appropriate funds for repairs, cleaning, heating, and lighting of these properties, including the jailer's residence if county-owned. The jailer is also empowered to take civil action to recover possession or damages for county property. The act excludes counties with circuit courts of continuous session or cities of the second class.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administrative duties of county jailers and the appropriation of county funds for the maintenance of public buildings. It does not contain any explicit or implicit racial classifications or restrictions. The language is purely administrative and financial, focusing on the upkeep of county property. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act grants Fiscal Courts in counties with a second-class city the authority to levy taxes for the maintenance, repair, cleanliness, sanitation, heating, lighting, and water supply of courthouses, courtrooms, offices, and adjacent public grounds. classification.title: An Act to amend an Act entitled "An Act to define the duties of said Association and regulating the practice of dentistry in this State," approved March 17, 1904.
classification.summary: This statute amends existing legislation regarding the practice of dentistry. It prohibits practicing dentistry under a company name unless established for fifteen years or more, requiring individuals to practice under their own names. It also mandates that each practitioner display their State Board certificate. Furthermore, it grants the State Board of Dental Examiners the power to refuse, suspend, or revoke certificates for reasons such as fraudulent or illegally obtained credentials, practice from unrecognized institutions, deception during examinations, criminal operations, felony convictions involving moral turpitude, chronic inebriety, drug addiction, or grossly unprofessional conduct likely to deceive the public.
classification.reasoning: The statute focuses on regulating the practice of dentistry, including requirements for individual practice names, display of certificates, and grounds for refusal or revocation of licenses. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' and the year 1908.

classification.title: AN ACT for the government and regulation of the Common Schools of the State.
classification.summary: This act establishes that each county will compose one school district, with an exception for cities or towns that maintain a separate system of public schools. The remaining part of the county outside such a city or town district will constitute a school district. Within thirty days of the act taking effect, county officials will divide each county school district into educational divisions with an equal number of school-aged children, including both white and colored children, based on the last census.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions dividing school districts to include 'both white and colored children'. This indicates a racial classification within the education system. While it doesn't mandate separate schools, the explicit mention of racial categories in the division of school districts strongly suggests a Jim Crow-era approach to education, likely leading to de facto or de jure segregation in the division of resources or student populations. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Section 4367 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to the Organization of School Districts
classification.summary: This act outlines the process for establishing and re-organizing school districts. It mandates that the boundaries of educational divisions and sub-districts should follow existing school districts for white children as closely as practicable. While it states that sub-districts shall include both white and colored children, it imposes minimum requirements for white children (fifty) and white pupil children for per capita apportionment (fifty) and teacher allocation (one hundred). Graded common school districts operating under special charters or municipal districts with local taxes are exempt.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white children' and 'colored children' in the context of school district boundaries and per capita apportionment. The requirement to follow 'existing school districts for white children' and the minimum number of 'white children' and 'white pupil children' for apportionment and teacher allocation demonstrate a clear racial segregation and preferential treatment within the education system, which is characteristic of Jim Crow laws. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' from the header 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' and the year 1908.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Section 4372 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to the Election of School Trustees.
classification.summary: This act outlines the process for electing school trustees in Kentucky. It specifies the election date, time, eligibility requirements for candidates and voters, nomination procedures, and ballot format. Candidates must be over 21, a resident of the sub-district for sixty days, and able to read and write, with a certificate from five reputable citizens. Male persons over 21, who have resided in the sub-division for sixty days, are eligible to vote. Elections are by ballot, printed and furnished by the county clerk, and nominations are by petition of at least ten eligible voters.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the election process for school trustees. It specifies requirements for candidates and voters, nomination procedures, and ballot specifications. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The requirements for residency, age, and literacy are applied universally. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in 1908. The category is 'education' because it pertains to the election of school officials. The confidence is high because the text clearly lacks any racial indicators.

classification.title: An Act to amend and re-enact Section 4368 of the Kentucky Statutes, relating to county high schools.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the establishment and classification of county high schools in Kentucky. It details the process for county boards of education to contract for the establishment and maintenance of high schools, including stipulations for teacher employment, curriculum, expenses, and pupil discipline. It specifies that the first county high school in a county should be located at the county seat if no suitable high school already exists there. The statute also defines three classes of high schools based on the length of their study courses (four, three, or two years) and mandates that county boards of education estimate educational needs and lay them before the fiscal court for tax levies to support schools. A proviso exempts property in cities and towns maintaining a first-class public school system from this tax levy.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses the establishment and funding of county high schools. It details the structure and curriculum of these schools and the process for levying taxes to support them. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The proviso exempting certain cities and towns from taxes is based on the quality of their existing school systems, not on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for levying taxes for local school purposes within school sub-districts. It specifies that collected funds are to be used for the building, improvement, and equipment of school houses, purchase of real estate, payment of teachers, purchasing supplies, and extending school terms for both white and colored pupils. The County Superintendent acts as treasurer and must provide a special bond. Funds are disbursed by order of the county board. A tax can be voted upon by legal voters of a sub-district, not exceeding twenty-five cents per one hundred dollars of taxable property, with notice posted fifteen days prior to the election held on the first Saturday in August.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions the allocation of funds for 'white and colored pupils,' indicating a racially segregated educational system. This is a hallmark of Jim Crow laws in the context of education. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' from 1908. The confidence is high because of the explicit mention of racial categories for educational services. The category is 'education' due to the subject matter. The racial indicator is 'explicit' because the terms 'white' and 'colored' are directly used.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 113 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to Common Schools
classification.summary: This act allows county boards to consolidate contiguous school sub-districts for either white or colored children. It also outlines requirements for building schoolhouses in consolidated districts and states that teachers shall be employed in the same manner as for sub-districts. Additionally, it prohibits trustees, county board members, and county superintendents from having financial interests in school-related contracts.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white or colored children' in the context of consolidating school sub-districts. This direct reference to racial categories in the organization of education strongly indicates a Jim Crow law. The law permits the consolidation of districts based on race, which is a form of segregation in education. The confidence is high due to the explicit mention of race. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in 1908. The category is 'education' as it deals with school districts and consolidation.

classification.title: AN ACT requiring cities of the first class to levy and collect a tax of not less than thirty-six (36) cents on each one hundred ($100) dollars worth of property assessed for taxation for city purposes for the maintenance of the Common Schools in said cities, being an amendment to Section 2969 of the Kentucky Statutes which is a part of an Act for the government of cities of the first class. Approved July 1, 1893.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 2969 of the Kentucky Statutes, which governs cities of the first class. It requires these cities to levy and collect a tax of at least thirty-six cents per one hundred dollars of assessed property for the maintenance of common schools. The amendment changes the year for this requirement from 1893 to 1909 and updates the minimum tax rate.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the funding of common schools in cities of the first class through property taxes. It is an amendment to an existing act and does not contain any explicit or implicit racial indicators. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the text clearly outlines a financial and administrative matter related to education funding without any mention or implication of race.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to establish a system of State Normal Schools in Kentucky"
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law establishing State Normal Schools in Kentucky. It divides the state into two districts, Eastern and Western, for the Eastern Kentucky State Normal School (Richmond) and the Western Kentucky State Normal School (Bowling Green). The boundaries of these districts are to be determined by a commission based on white population, aiming for districts that are as equal as possible in white population.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions dividing the state into two normal school districts and states that these districts 'shall always be as near equal as may be in white population.' This implies a racial basis for the division and potentially for the student body or faculty of these schools, which is a characteristic of Jim Crow era segregation in education. While it doesn't explicitly state separate schools for different races, the focus on 'white population' for district equality strongly suggests a racially motivated structure. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' and amends a prior act. The confidence is high due to the explicit mention of 'white population' in the context of district division for educational institutions. Human review is not strictly necessary as the implication is clear enough for classification.

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 14 of an Act of March 21, 1906, relating to educational certificates.
classification.summary: This act repeals Section 14 of a previous act and enacts new provisions regarding educational certificates. It outlines three types of certificates: Elementary, Intermediate, and Advanced, which are awarded upon completion of one, two, and three years of work, respectively. These certificates grant holders the right to teach in public schools for specified periods without further examination. The Advanced Certificate can be extended for life upon demonstration of successful teaching and good moral character. The endorsement of the State Superintendent of Public Instruction is required to validate all certificates and extensions.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the requirements and validity of teaching certificates in Kentucky's public schools. It establishes different levels of certificates based on educational attainment and outlines the process for their issuance and extension. There is no mention of race or any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it refers to amending a previous act from a specific date (March 21, 1906) and is presented as an act of the General Assembly in 1908.

classification.title: An Act to amend an Act approved March 21, 1906, relating to Normal Schools.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning Normal Schools. It establishes a system where each county is entitled to appoint a certain number of white pupils for free tuition based on the number of white schoolchildren in the county. The selection process involves county superintendents examining applicants and selecting those with the highest qualifications and good character. Vacancies can be filled by alternates.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white pupil' and bases appointments on the number of 'white schoolchildren'. This direct racial classification in the context of educational access is a hallmark of Jim Crow legislation, which enforced racial segregation and discrimination. The statute's focus on 'white' students for free tuition, without any mention or provision for other racial groups, clearly indicates a discriminatory intent and practice. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it refers to an 'Act approved March 21, 1906' and is presented as part of the General Assembly's proceedings for 1908. classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning State Normal Schools. It grants the Board of Regents the power to require pupils to pay fees and tuition if they refuse to sign a declaration to teach in public schools for a specified period after graduation. It also grants the Board of Regents the power to purchase additional real estate, condemn land for school purposes, and lease or donate land for library construction. Furthermore, it allows the Board to erect or lease buildings and grounds, and to sell and reinvest proceeds from real estate or buildings.

classification.summary: This act prohibits children under sixteen from working in hazardous occupations and machinery, and outlines reporting and inspection procedures for labor inspectors and truant officers. It specifies various dangerous tasks and machinery that children are forbidden to operate or assist with, including sewing belts, cleaning machinery, operating saws, planers, steam boilers, dough brakes, rolling mill machinery, and working with dangerous acids or in paint/white lead manufacturing.

There is no mention of race or any provisions that would disproportionately affect Black Kentuckians. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'GenAssemb 1908.pdf' and the context of legislative acts. It prohibits their employment in the manufacture of goods for immoral purposes, in places of amusement where intoxicating liquors are sold, and mandates that females under sixteen do not have to remain standing constantly. It also prohibits employing children under sixteen in occupations dangerous to health, morals, or limbs, with the county physician or city health officer's decision being final. The act further requires employers to provide safety guards for dangerous machinery and suitable, clean washrooms and closets for employees under sixteen.

A 'delinquent child' is defined as a child who violates any law, is incorrigible, associates with criminals, absents themselves from home without consent, is growing up in idleness or crime, or knowingly visits houses of ill repute.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the definition and treatment of dependent, neglected, and delinquent children. It sets age limits for applicability and defines what constitutes a delinquent child. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in 1908. The confidence is high because the statute's content is clearly focused on child welfare and not racial segregation or discrimination.

classification.title: Dependent or neglected child defined
classification.summary: This statute defines what constitutes a 'delinquent child' and a 'dependent child' or 'neglected child' for the purposes of the act. It outlines behaviors such as patronizing policy shops, saloons, pool-rooms, wandering at night, riding trains without authority, using indecent language, or being persistently truant as indicators of delinquency. It also defines dependent or neglected children as those begging, singing for gain, lacking a home or proper guardianship, being destitute, abandoned, or lacking proper parental care, or whose home is unfit due to parental depravity.
classification.reasoning: The statute defines terms related to child welfare and delinquency. It does not contain any explicit or implicit racial classifications or restrictions. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' and the year 1908. classification.summary: This act establishes exclusive jurisdiction for county courts over cases involving dependent and neglected children. It outlines procedures for jury trials, mandates a special record book titled 'The Juvenile Record' and a 'The Juvenile Docket', and requires a special courtroom in counties with first-class cities for juvenile cases. The act also defines terms like 'association' and 'child' and states that evidence from these proceedings cannot be used in other civil or criminal cases against the child.
classification.reasoning: The statute text provided deals with the establishment and procedures for juvenile courts and the handling of dependent and neglected children. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The focus is on the legal framework for child welfare cases. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act Concerning Proceedings in Chambers and Release of Children on Recognizance
classification.summary: This statute outlines procedures for serving summons and notices related to child welfare cases, including provisions for warrants if summons cannot be served or is ineffectual. It also details that hearings should be held in chambers, to the exclusion of the general public, and that a child may be released on their own recognizance or that of their parent or guardian until the first hearing.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with procedural aspects of child welfare cases, including service of process, court hearings, and release of children. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The focus is on the legal process for handling children in need of court intervention. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Section 331 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to the Care and Custody of Delinquent, Dependent and Neglected Children.
classification.summary: This act amends statutes concerning the care of delinquent, dependent, or neglected children. It prohibits the incarceration of children under fourteen in county jails or police stations and outlines provisions for detention schools, including requirements for their operation and staffing. It also specifies that in counties with a first-class city and a Board of Children's Guardians, a provided temporary home shall be used as a detention school. In other counties or those with a second-class city, the fiscal court must establish and maintain a detention school.
classification.reasoning: The statute focuses on the treatment and detention of children, specifically prohibiting the incarceration of young children in jails and establishing guidelines for detention schools. While it deals with the justice system and child welfare, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it refers to amending existing statutes and is presented as an act from the General Assembly in 1908.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Section 331 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to Juvenile Courts and Probation Officers
classification.summary: This statute outlines the powers of the court regarding delinquent, neglected, or dependent children. It details provisions for probation, placement in family homes, or commitment to institutions like the House of Reform for Boys or Girls. The court can remove a child from parental custody if parents are unfit or unable to care for them, or if it's in the child's best interest. Children committed are to be trained and educated. The statute specifies that a child cannot be committed beyond the age of twenty-one.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with juvenile justice and child welfare. It outlines procedures for handling delinquent, neglected, or dependent children, including probation, foster care, and institutionalization. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The focus is solely on the welfare and legal status of children. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It details conditions under which a child can be removed from parental custody, placed in foster homes, or committed to associations. A specific provision states that all female children committed to or released from any institution must be accompanied by a woman attendant. The court also has the power to return a child to its former home if deemed suitable.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the welfare and custody of children, specifically neglected, dependent, and delinquent ones. It outlines court procedures for removing children from parental care and placing them in alternative care or with associations. While this clause could be interpreted as a protective measure, there is no explicit or implicit racial component to the statute. The language used is general and applies to all children within the scope of the act. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law.

It details the process for identifying and addressing truancy, including notifying parents and proceeding against delinquent children and their guardians. The county court is given exclusive jurisdiction over these cases.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the appointment, duties, and jurisdiction of truant officers. It focuses on enforcing school attendance and addressing truancy. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'GenAssemb 1908.pdf' and the context of legislative acts. The confidence is high because the statute's content is clearly focused on educational enforcement without any racial component.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the compulsory education of children in cities of the first, second, third and fourth classes," approved March 15, 1904.
classification.summary: This act details the administrative and reporting requirements for truant officers and school superintendents in cities of the first, second, third, and fourth classes. It mandates the creation and maintenance of records by truant officers, their supervision by city superintendents, and the annual reporting of the act's workings. It also requires school superintendents to furnish principals with lists of children of school age (7-14) who are required to attend school, including their personal and parental information. Principals are then required to report daily absentees to the superintendent or truant officers.
classification.reasoning: The statute focuses on the administrative and enforcement mechanisms of compulsory education laws in specific classes of Kentucky cities. It outlines the duties of truant officers and school superintendents, including record-keeping and reporting. There is no mention of race or any provisions that would inherently discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' and the year 1908. The confidence is high because the text clearly describes administrative procedures without any racial component. The category is 'education' as it directly pertains to school operations and attendance.

classification.title: An Act to Amend an Act Entitled "An Act to Provide for the Compulsory Education of Children in Cities of the First and Second Class in this Commonwealth," Approved March 15, 1894, and to Provide for Parental or Truant Schools.
classification.summary: This act amends existing compulsory education laws for cities of the first and second class in Kentucky. It outlines the duties of truant officers in reporting and addressing student absences and truancy. It also authorizes the establishment and maintenance of parental or truant schools for children of compulsory school age who are neglected, dependent, or delinquent.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Section 204 of the Acts of the General Assembly, Relating to Parental or Truant Schools
classification.summary: This act amends existing legislation concerning parental or truant schools. It outlines conditions for the establishment of such schools, including their location relative to city limits and penal institutions. It also details regulations for religious instruction within these schools, ensuring it aligns with the beliefs of the children's parents or guardians. Furthermore, the act specifies procedures for releasing children on probation, including requirements for regular school attendance and satisfactory conduct for a year before final discharge.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses the establishment and operation of parental or truant schools, including provisions for religious instruction and probation for committed children. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The focus is on the educational and rehabilitative aspects of these institutions for children deemed delinquent or truant. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend section 4375 of the Kentucky Statutes, Carroll's Edition, 1903.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 4375 of the Kentucky Statutes by changing how accumulated school funds can be used. Previously, the money could only be used to extend the school term. The amendment allows the money to be used to either extend the school term or supplement teacher salaries, with the agreement of the district trustees and teachers, and requiring approval from the County Superintendent for unused funds from previous years.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an amendment to existing Kentucky Statutes concerning the use of school funds. It modifies how accumulated money can be allocated, either for extending the school term or for supplementing teacher salaries. There is no mention of race, nor any implicit racial distinctions in its provisions. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an Act of the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the text clearly indicates a financial and administrative change within the education system without any racial component.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to regulate burial associations, corporations and companies doing business in the State of Kentucky."
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law regulating burial associations. It requires these entities to deposit double the aggregate face value of their policies, bonds, or certificates with the State Treasurer. These funds must be invested in securities allowed for insurance companies and held in trust. The act also outlines procedures for receivership and asset distribution in case of discontinuation of business, dissolution, or unsatisfied judgments. Failure to comply with these provisions constitutes a misdemeanor, punishable by a fine.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financial regulation and operational procedures of burial associations. It mandates deposit requirements, investment of funds, and procedures for dissolution and receivership. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The amendment specifies timelines for clerk entries and the commissioner's actions.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 2380 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to Ditches
classification.summary: This act amends sections 20, 22, 23, and 24 of section 2380 of the Kentucky Statutes. It clarifies the duties of the County Judge in cases where a ditch is established in multiple counties, appoints a commissioner to maintain the ditch, and outlines the funding mechanism for the commissioner's salary and maintenance costs through assessments on lands originally assessed for construction. The assessment is capped at ten percent of the original assessment and collected by the sheriff.

It outlines the responsibilities of county judges and appointed commissioners, as well as the financial mechanisms for upkeep.
